ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for fattoush; A0FKP (mixed vegetable salad) used as closest parent. Verified against EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for fattoush as of 2024 release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fattoush weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical restaurant or home serving of this Levantine salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fattoush salad (composite estimate from raw vegetable components and olive oil dressing with toasted flatbread)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for fattoush as a composite dish. Values are derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for the individual components (romaine lettuce, tomato, cucumber, radish, bell pepper, spring onion, parsley, mint, olive oil, lemon juice, toasted pita bread) weighted by a standard recipe proportion. The input record's per-100g values (energy 93.33 kcal, protein 2.0 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 8.33 g) are consistent with this USDA component-based estimate and are retained. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a fattoush entry. ICMR-NIN and IMR MyFCD do not cover this Levantine dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed vegetable salad with flatbread
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fattoush exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the analogous composite of: raw mixed vegetables (GI ~15–30), olive oil dressing (negligible GI contribution), and toasted Arabic flatbread (GI ~57 per University of Sydney entry for pita bread, white). Weighted by approximate ingredient proportions (~60% vegetables, ~10% bread, ~30% dressing), the blended GI estimate is approximately 54 (low category). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (54 × 8.33 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 13.5, rounded to 12.0 after fibre adjustment. This estimate is conservative and consistent with published mixed-salad GI analogues.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (150 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium (~280 mg/100 g) from tomato, cucumber, bell pepper, and radish
hypertension.guidance: Fattoush is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its low sodium content and potassium-rich vegetable base aligned with DASH dietary principles; however, patients should request reduced added salt in the dressing and limit the toasted bread portion to minimise refined carbohydrate and any additional sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (8.33 g/100 g), low sugar (2.0 g/100 g), dietary fibre (1.67 g/100 g), and olive oil dressing slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fattoush has a low estimated glycemic index (~54) and low glycemic load per serving, making it appropriate for type 2 diabetes management; patients should limit the toasted Arabic bread component, which is a refined carbohydrate, and retain the sumac-lemon dressing as a low-GI flavour base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.83 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high monounsaturated fat from olive oil (~3.8 g/100 g), and antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fattoush supports cardiovascular health through its olive oil-based dressing providing cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and oleocanthal, combined with fibre-rich vegetables and vitamin C (13.33 mg/100 g); the low saturated fat and zero trans fat profile is consistent with AHA dietary recommendations for CVD risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers) containing solanine alkaloids that may trigger flares in sensitive RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fattoush contains nightshade vegetables (tomatoes and bell peppers) whose solanine content may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; patients should request the salad without nightshades while retaining the olive oil dressing and herbs for their anti-inflammatory oleocanthal and polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fattoush (also spelled Fattouch, Fattush, or Fattouche) is a classic Levantine bread salad originating from the eastern Mediterranean, particularly Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine, and widely served across the Middle East and in Middle Eastern restaurants globally. The salad is built on a colourful base of chopped fresh vegetables — typically romaine or cos lettuce, ripe tomatoes, cucumbers, radishes, bell peppers, spring onions, fresh parsley, and mint — dressed with a tangy vinaigrette of extra-virgin olive oil, freshly squeezed lemon juice, sumac (a tart, fruity red spice ground from dried sumac berries), garlic, and salt. The defining textural element is crispy pieces of toasted or fried Arabic flatbread (khubz), which are torn or cut and scattered over the salad just before serving to provide a satisfying crunch that contrasts with the juicy vegetables. The flavour profile is bright, acidic, herbaceous, and slightly earthy from the sumac. Nutritionally, fattoush is a low-calorie, low-fat salad at approximately 93 kcal per 100 g, with 6 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fat from olive oil), 8.33 g carbohydrate, 2 g protein, and 1.67 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 150 mg/100 g. The dish provides vitamin C (13.33 mg/100 g) from bell peppers and tomatoes, calcium (26.67 mg/100 g), and iron (0.67 mg/100 g). Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 54 places it in the low-GI category, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, fattoush is appropriate for hypertension (low sodium, potassium-rich vegetables aligned with DASH principles), type 2 diabetes (low GI, low available carbohydrate, fibre and olive oil moderating glucose response), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, zero trans fat, olive oil MUFAs). Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade alkaloids should note the presence of tomatoes and bell peppers. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and halal, but contains gluten from the toasted flatbread.
common_user_queries: What bread is used in fattoush?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD) carries a fattoush entry. Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central component-based estimation across standard fattoush ingredients. The input record values are consistent with this estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney pita bread and mixed vegetable data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is robust and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for romaine lettuce, tomato, cucumber, radish, bell pepper, spring onion, parsley, mint, olive oil, lemon juice, and toasted pita bread, weighted by standard fattoush recipe proportions. The input record per-100g values are consistent with this estimate and are preserved. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g) are estimated from USDA olive oil fatty acid profile applied to the ~30% olive oil dressing fraction; these are approximations. 3. potassium_mg (280 mg) is estimated from USDA vegetable component data; not directly measured for this composite. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae (38 mcg) is estimated from tomato and bell pepper USDA entries. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as fattoush is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no animal or fortified ingredients are present. 7. Fields set to -1 (data not available from USDA component estimation for this composite): vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; individual component data exists but reliable composite weighting for these micronutrients requires laboratory analysis. 8. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for fattoush exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 9. The input record lists 'country_of_origin: Singapore' reflecting the serving context (Singapore restaurant); the dish's true culinary origin is the Levant and this is reflected in region_of_origin. 10. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean serving context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules, as this dish is served in Singapore. 11. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest analogous match for mixed vegetable salad; no dedicated fattoush code exists in EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2.
